data.title: Electric vehicle sales soar in India amid concerns about emissions rules, gas prices
data.description:
Electric vehicle sales in India got a recent boost after concerns about new emission rules and higher fuel costs from the Iran conflict made more buyers consider non-gasoline options. Sales rose 82% year over year to 24,148 units during March, and full-year sales climbed 87% to 233,246, according to JATO Dynamics. 

While higher gas prices due to the Iran-Israel-U.S. conflict were a factor, the bigger concern for Indian car owners is upcoming emissions rules, which will force automakers to cut average fleet carbon emissions sharply starting next April and even more by 2032.

Despite the notable jump in sales this year, EVs still account for only about 5% of India's total car market, held back by a lack of affordable models, weak charging infrastructure, poor resale value, and range anxiety. EV sales in India remain smaller in terms of market share than those in Thailand, Vietnam, and Indonesia, even though they are growing quickly in absolute terms.

Automakers are now accelerating EV plans because the policy direction is becoming clearer, and analysts expect EV market share to reach around 10% by 2028 and reach about 19% by 2032. That could push annual EV sales to roughly 500K in a few years and eventually to 1 million, making electric offerings harder to ignore for manufacturers.

Currently, the EV market in India is still dominated by Tata Motors, Mahindra (MAHMF), and JSW MG Motor, which together accounted for nearly 85% of March EV sales, but new entrants and partnerships are lining up as well, including Honda Motor's (HMC) first India EV tests and JSW Group's talks with Chinese automaker Chery. Meanwhile, Tesla (TSLA) formally entered India in July 2025 with its first showroom in Mumbai and the Model Y, but it launched at a premium price because the cars are imported and face steep duties. The Austin-based company remains a niche premium player rather than a mass-market force in India.

data.title: Ulta set to capitalize on bullish makeup cycle -- Jefferies
data.description:
“The importance of makeup cannot be overstated,” said Jefferies analyst Sydney Wagner, “and remains the highest-velocity category in beauty and 38% of ULTA sales.” With Ulta Beauty (ULTA) better positioned to capitalize on renewed makeup engagement and with a more favorable risk/reward, Wagner upgrades the stock to Buy from Hold and lifts the target price for the stock by 26% to $700.  

What differentiates the current beauty environment from previous, short-lived makeup trends is the growing alignment between consumer interest and supply-side response.  This encourages brands to increase innovation while retailers expand resets, assortments, and “shelf emphasis.”

Wagner believes this combination materially increases the likelihood that early demand will translate into sustained momentum, consistent with the early stages of a true category cycle rather than “episodic spikes in engagement.” 

Accordingly, Ulta Beauty (ULTA) recently changed its merchandise assortment, shifting from “catch-up” and from brands largely available on Amazon (AMZN) to offensive with more relevant brands and trend-led launches. And since this change, Ulta (ULTA) has improved the pace and relevance of new brand additions, moving beyond “gap-filling” to proactive.

“Ulta’s position as the industry’s most comprehensive, cross-price beauty destination is becoming more relevant as momentum builds across sub-categories,” Wagner writes, adding that as the makeup cycle gains traction, Ulta (ULTA) is better positioned to convert category momentum into “sustained shares and earnings durability.”

Wall Street analysts and Seeking Alpha authors are also bullish on Ulta (ULTA) with an average Buy rating for the stock. Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating, however, is more cautious with a score of 2.99 out of 5, translating into a Hold rating based on low scores for its growth and valuation metrics.

Ulta Beauty (ULTA) shares are set up to trade in the green for a sixth consecutive day.

data.title: VNET Group CFO to step down
data.description:
• VNET Group (VNET) chief financial officer Qiyu Wang will resign effective April 30, 2026.
  • Peter Zhihua Zhang, appointed SVP of Operational Finance in February 2026, will continue overseeing financial operations.

data.title: AI chip startups near record funding to challenge Nvidia, CNBC's Schwarz says
data.description:
AI chip startups are on pace to shatter funding records in 2026 as they position themselves to challenge Nvidia's (NVDA) dominance in the artificial intelligence semiconductor market

CNBC’s Kai Nicol-Schwarz said companies developing alternative chip technology have already raised ~$8.3 billion this year, approaching the previous record of $8.5 billion.

The largest funding rounds have come from U.S.-based startups, with Cerebras leading the pack after securing $1 billion in February. Other notable raises include Etched and MatX, which each picked up $500 million this year.

European startups are also attracting significant investment, with UK-based Olyx and Netherlands-based Etcetera both completing rounds exceeding $200 million.

The surge in funding reflects a fundamental shift in how AI technology is being deployed. The industry is transitioning from training AI models—the process of programming data into them—toward inference, which involves the day-to-day application of these models.

“AI is moving from this point of training these AI models towards how you actually use them,” Schwarz explained, noting that inference encompasses “how you build applications, how you power tools running on these models.”

Startups are betting that Nvidia’s chips, while currently dominant, aren’t optimally designed for this emerging use case. Nvidia originally developed its semiconductors for the gaming sector before they were repurposed for AI applications. Rival chip companies argue this creates an opportunity for purpose-built alternatives.

“The argument of a lot of these chip startups is that these chips weren’t originally designed for AI inference,” the expert noted.

These companies contend that “novel system architectures” are needed to “more effectively, more efficiently run these AI models.”

While Nvidia remains the undisputed leader in AI chip technology, the record-breaking investment flowing into competitors suggests the market believes there is room for alternatives as AI applications become increasingly mainstream.

data.title: Rayonier Advanced Materials launches strategic review, CEO resigns
data.description:
Rayonier Advanced Materials Inc. (RYAM) on Monday said it began a formal review of strategic alternatives aimed at increasing shareholder value, while also announcing the immediate resignation of President and Chief Executive Officer Scott Sutton. Its shares slipped 2% in premarket trading. 

The company said it has hired Morgan Stanley & Co. LLC as financial adviser and Wachtell, Lipton, Rosen & Katz as legal counsel to assist with the review.

RYAM said the process will examine a range of options, including a possible sale of all or part of the company, a strategic investment, merger, other business combination or continuation of its standalone strategy.

The company said the review follows unsolicited expressions of interest it recently received.

Following Sutton’s departure, the board created an interim Office of the Chief Executive Officer to oversee operations during the transition. The group includes Chief Financial Officer Marcus J. Moeltner, Vice President of Manufacturing Operations Michael Osborne, Senior Vice President of Biomaterials Christian Ribeyrolle and General Counsel R. Colby Slaughter.

Board Chair Lisa M. Palumbo said the company is also working with an executive search firm to identify a permanent chief executive.

RYAM said a committee of the board will support the interim leadership team during the strategic review.

The company said it has not set a timetable for completing the process and may not provide further updates unless disclosure is required or deemed appropriate. It added there is no assurance the review will lead to a transaction or strategic change.

data.title: VNET Group CFO to step down
data.description:
• VNET Group (VNET) chief financial officer Qiyu Wang will resign effective April 30, 2026.
  • Peter Zhihua Zhang, appointed SVP of Operational Finance in February 2026, will continue overseeing financial operations.

data.title: NXT Energy Solutions appoints new CEO
data.description:
• NXT Energy Solutions (NSFDF) has appointed Gerry Sheehan as chief executive officer effective April 20, 2026.
  • Sheehan, a director since 2021, brings 40 years of experience in oil and gas exploration, geophysics, and global energy projects.
  • He previously spent nearly 20 years at Tullow Oil Plc, including as International Exploration Manager.
  • Sheehan succeeds Bruce G. Wilcox, who is retiring as CEO but will remain on the board.
  • The leadership change marks NXT’s “third stage” focused on expanding services, geographic reach, and client base.

data.title: Surf Air Mobility raises $15M equity, secures $15M loan; shares down
data.description:
• Surf Air Mobility (SRFM) is issuing 13.3M shares at $1.10 each to institutional investors in a registered direct offering.
  • Company insiders (directors and officers) will buy an additional 257,353 shares at $1.36 per share (April 17 closing price).
  • Total gross proceeds from the equity offering are expected to be about $15M.
  • The offering is scheduled to close around April 21, 2026.
  • Proceeds will fund SurfOS software rollout, electrification initiatives, and/or debt repayment.
  • The company also secured a separate non-dilutive $15M promissory note backed by aircraft equity.
  • The loan carries a 12.5% interest rate, payable monthly in cash or stock at the company’s discretion.
  • SRFM shares fell 7.7% premarket.

data.title: Apollo to acquire ~13% of McKesson's Medical-Surgical Solutions business
data.description:
Apollo Global Management (APO) affiliated funds are set to acquire a minority ownership interest of ~13% in McKesson's (MCK) Medical-Surgical Solutions unit.

The funds will invest $1.25B in convertible preferred equity of the unit.

The deal values Medical-Surgical Solutions at about $13B.

"This transaction marks a key milestone in McKesson's planned separation of MMS," said McKesson CEO Brian Tyler.

APO was 1.66% lower Monday pre-market to $122.55, while MCK was +0.30% to $869.00.

data.title: Pentagon rift between Hegseth and Army chief raises leadership questions
data.description:
A growing feud between Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Army Secretary Dan Driscoll has spilled into public view, highlighting internal friction at the Pentagon after the abrupt firing of Army Chief of Staff Gen. Randy George, The Wall Street Journal reported Monday.

The clash comes as the U.S. military faces multiple global demands, making stability at the top of the Defense Department a key issue for investors tracking defense spending, procurement priorities and contractor execution risk.

The dispute reportedly began soon after both men took office in 2025, when Hegseth sharply rebuked Driscoll over a proposal involving President Trump and Vice President JD Vance. Tensions later deepened amid controversies surrounding Hegseth and disagreements over promotions, staffing and Army leadership decisions.

The conflict became public during a congressional hearing when Driscoll praised George, who Hegseth dismissed earlier this month while Driscoll was away on vacation. Lawmakers from both parties criticized how George’s departure was handled and praised his service.

According to people familiar with the matter, Hegseth had also pushed to remove certain officers from a promotion list, including women, Black officers and officials tied to former Joint Chiefs Chairman Mark Milley. Driscoll reportedly resisted those demands.

The leadership turmoil could matter for investors because the Army is in the middle of modernization efforts involving weapons systems, logistics and training programs. Management instability can delay procurement decisions, complicate budget planning and create uncertainty for defense contractors reliant on Army contracts.

The White House said President Trump supports both officials, while Pentagon spokesman Sean Parnell said Hegseth maintains strong relationships across the military services, the Journal reported.
